section.title: Greeting and Thanksgiving
section.sectionRationale: The opening sets the warm, joyful tone of the whole letter. Paul and Timothy greet the saints at Philippi, and Paul pours out thanks and prayer for a church that has partnered with him in the gospel from the first day. Two great confidences frame everything that follows: that God who began a good work will finish it, and that Paul carries this church in his heart with the very affection of Christ.
section.thesis: Paul greets a beloved church with grace and peace, thanks God for their partnership in the gospel, rests in the confidence that God will complete the good work He began in them, and prays that their love would abound in knowledge until they are filled with the fruit of righteousness to the glory of God.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the greeting to the saints with their overseers and deacons; thanksgiving for their partnership and the confidence that God finishes what He begins; and Paul's prayer that their love would grow into discernment and the fruit of righteousness.

units.label: Greeting
units.note: grace and peace to the saints at Philippi

verses.text: Paul and Timothy, servants of Christ Jesus, To all the {{hagioi|saints}} in Christ Jesus at Philippi, together with the overseers and deacons:
Grace and peace to you from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

units.label: Thanksgiving and confidence
units.note: partnership in the gospel, a work God will finish

verses.text: I thank my God every time I remember you.
In every prayer for all of you, I always pray with joy,
because of your {{koinonia|partnership}} in the gospel from the first day until now,
being confident of this, that He who began a good work in you will {{epiteleo|carry it on to completion}} until the day of Christ Jesus.
It is right for me to feel this way about all of you, since I have you in my heart. For in my chains and in my defense and confirmation of the gospel, you are all partners in grace with me.
God is my witness how I long for all of you with the {{splanchna|affection}} of Christ Jesus.

units.label: Paul's prayer
units.note: love abounding in knowledge unto the fruit of righteousness

verses.text: And this is my prayer: that your love may abound more and more in knowledge and depth of insight,
so that you may be able to {{dokimazo|test and prove}} what is best and may be pure and blameless for the day of Christ,
filled with the fruit of righteousness that comes through Jesus Christ, to the glory and praise of God.

hagioi.gloss: Holy ones, saints, those set apart for God. Paul does not reserve the word for a spiritual elite; every believer in Christ at Philippi is a saint, made holy not by achievement but by being in Christ Jesus. The whole church, with its overseers and deacons, is addressed as God's set apart people.

koinonia.gloss: Fellowship, partnership, sharing in common. It is one of the key words of the letter. The Philippians are not merely spectators of Paul's mission but true partners in it, sharing in the gospel through prayer, gifts, and suffering from the first day until now. Their partnership is with Paul and, deeper still, with Christ and His gospel.

epiteleo.gloss: To bring fully to completion, to finish what was started. God is the one who began the good work of salvation and God is the one who will carry it through to the end. The word grounds Christian assurance not in the believer's staying power but in God's faithfulness to complete His own work by the day of Christ.

splanchna.gloss: The inward parts, the seat of deep affection; a yearning felt in the gut. Paul longs for the Philippians not with mere sentiment but with the very affection of Christ Jesus, as though Christ's own heart of love beats through him toward this church.

dokimazo.gloss: To test, examine, and approve what is genuine after proving it, as metals are assayed. Paul prays that growing love and knowledge will give the Philippians the discernment to test and approve what is best, so that they are pure and blameless for the day of Christ.

basic.context: Paul writes from prison to a church he loves dearly, the first he planted in Europe. He opens not with complaint but with thanks and joy. The Philippians have stood with him in the gospel from the very beginning, sharing his work through prayer, partnership, and gifts. Paul is sure of one thing above all: the God who started His saving work in them will bring it to completion, and so he prays their love will keep on growing.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the greeting and thanksgiving of a real letter, warm and personal, yet every line carries gospel weight. The joy, the confidence, and the prayer here set the themes for the whole book. We read it both as Paul's word to Philippi and, through it, as God's encouragement to every church that partners in the gospel.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul calls them all hagioi, saints, holy ones, not an elite few but the whole church in Christ. He thanks God for their koinonia, their partnership in the gospel. He is confident God will epiteleo, fully complete, the work He began. He longs for them with the splanchna, the deep gut affection, of Christ. And he prays they would dokimazo, test and approve, what is best.
basic.typology: The fruit of righteousness Paul prays for gathers up the Old Testament hope of a people who bear good fruit for God, the vine and the planting of the Lord. What the law could not produce, the Spirit now brings through Jesus Christ, so that the harvest is to God's own glory and praise.
basic.christ: Everything here is in Christ Jesus: the saints are in Him, grace and peace come through Him, the good work is completed by His day, Paul's affection is His affection, and the fruit of righteousness comes through Him. Christ is the source, the substance, and the goal of the Christian life from first day to last.
basic.god: God is the giver of grace and peace, the one Paul thanks, the faithful one who begins and finishes the good work, and the one to whom all glory and praise are due. He is a God who saves by His own initiative and guarantees the outcome, so that assurance rests on His character, not ours.
basic.crossReferences: Philippians 2:13 says it is God who works in you. 1 Thessalonians 5:24 says the God who calls you is faithful and will do it. John 15 speaks of bearing much fruit as branches in the vine, Jesus.
basic.oneStory: God has always been gathering a people for Himself and pledging to keep them to the end. Here that great story lands on a small church in Philippi: those He has made saints in Christ He will carry all the way to the day of Christ, bearing fruit for His glory.

context.literary: The passage follows the standard shape of an ancient letter opening, sender, recipients, greeting, then a thanksgiving and prayer, but Paul fills the form with gospel content. The thanksgiving in verses 3 to 8 flows into an intercessory prayer in verses 9 to 11, and the themes it sounds, joy, partnership, confidence, the day of Christ, run through the entire letter.
context.historical: Philippi was a Roman colony in Macedonia, proud of its citizenship, where Paul first preached in Europe, with Lydia and the Philippian jailer among the earliest converts. Paul writes as a prisoner, most likely in Rome, yet the tone is thankful and warm, for this church had repeatedly sent him aid and stood by him.
context.canonical: The letter belongs with Paul's prison epistles. Its opening confidence, that God completes what He begins, states in miniature the biblical doctrine of God's persevering grace, a thread that runs from the covenant promises through Romans 8 to the final gathering of the redeemed.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To greet a beloved church, to express heartfelt thanks and joy for their gospel partnership, to assure them that God will finish the work He began in them, and to pray that their love would grow into discernment and holiness that glorify God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The thanksgiving describes Paul's genuine feeling for this specific church, yet the truths it rests on are prescriptive for all: God completes His work, and love is meant to abound in knowledge and bear the fruit of righteousness. The confidence of verse 6 is a promise to be trusted, not merely Paul's private optimism.

debates.question: What is the good work God will complete, and how sure is the outcome?

views.label: God's saving work in the believer
views.case: The good work is the salvation God began at their conversion, and He pledges to bring it to completion at the day of Christ; assurance rests on God's faithfulness, not human effort.

views.label: Their partnership in the gospel
views.case: Some link the good work to the Philippians' active partnership just mentioned in verse 5; yet even this is God's own doing in them, so the confidence still rests on Him.

debates.bearing: Either way the guarantee is God's; the verse anchors Christian assurance in the character of the one who starts and finishes salvation.

debates.question: Does Paul address overseers and deacons as fixed church offices?

views.label: Recognized leaders in the church
views.case: The overseers and deacons are named alongside the saints as the church's servant leaders, an early witness to ordered ministry in the congregation.

views.label: Simply the whole church served by its helpers
views.case: The emphasis falls on all the saints; the leaders are greeted with them, not set above them, reflecting servant leadership rather than hierarchy.

debates.bearing: The passage honors leadership while keeping the whole body, all the saints, at the center of the greeting.

study.originalLanguages: The greeting names the whole church as hagioi, saints, holy ones set apart in Christ, not a special class but every believer. The heart of the thanksgiving is koinonia, partnership or fellowship, one of the letter's signature words: the Philippians share in the gospel with Paul through prayer, gifts, and suffering. Paul's confidence turns on epiteleo, to complete fully, God will finish the good work He began. His longing is described with splanchna, the inward parts, the deep visceral affection of Christ working through him. And his prayer aims at dokimazo, the ability to test and approve what is best, the fruit of a love that grows in knowledge and depth of insight.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfills the Old Testament longing for a people who bear good fruit for God, the vine and planting of the Lord, now produced through Jesus Christ to God's glory.
Takes up the prophets' day of the Lord and centers it on Jesus, the day when His people are found pure and blameless and God's work in them is complete.

study.christ: Christ Jesus saturates the opening. The saints are in Him, grace and peace flow from Him alongside the Father, the good work will be completed by His day, Paul longs for the church with His very affection, and the fruit of righteousness comes through Him to God's glory. Christ is not one theme among many but the atmosphere in which the whole church lives and the one to whose day everything moves.
study.god: God is the giver of grace and peace, the object of Paul's constant thanks, and above all the faithful worker who begins and completes salvation. The confidence of verse 6 reveals a God who does not abandon what He starts. He is the one who deserves all glory and praise, and the growth of His people in love and righteousness is finally for His honor, not merely their improvement.

crossReferences.note: God is the one who works in believers to will and to act, the ground of the confidence in verse 6.
The faithful God who calls you will sanctify you completely and will surely do it, echoing the promise to complete the work.
Those God foreknew He also glorifies, the unbroken chain behind the confidence that He finishes what He begins.
Branches in the vine bear much fruit, the fruit of righteousness Paul prays for here.

study.oneStory: From Abraham onward God has taken the initiative to save a people and pledged to keep them to the end. That covenant faithfulness now rests on a colony church in Philippi: God who began His good work in them will carry it through to the day of Christ, and their growing, fruitful love points to the harvest of the whole redeemed people gathered to God's praise.

quiz.q: Why is Paul so confident about the Philippians' future?

options.text: Because God who began a good work in them will carry it to completion.
options.note: Verse 6 grounds assurance in God's faithfulness to finish His own work.

options.text: Because the Philippians are strong enough to finish on their own.
options.note: The confidence rests on God's work, not on their strength.

options.text: Because they have wealthy patrons to support them.
options.note: The ground is God's completing work, not human resources.

options.text: Because no trouble will ever come to them.
options.note: Paul writes from chains; the confidence is about God finishing, not a trouble free life.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by the Philippians' partnership in the gospel?

options.text: Their shared fellowship in the gospel work through prayer, gifts, and suffering.
options.note: Koinonia means true partnership from the first day until now.

options.text: Their agreement to admire Paul from a distance.
options.note: It is active partnership, not mere admiration.

options.text: A business contract for mutual profit.
options.note: The sharing is in the gospel, not commercial gain.

options.text: Their decision to keep the gospel to themselves.
options.note: Partnership means sharing in the gospel's spread, not hoarding it.

quiz.q: What does Paul pray for the Philippians?

options.text: That their love would abound in knowledge and insight, bearing the fruit of righteousness.
options.note: Verses 9 to 11 pray for love that grows into discernment and holiness.

options.text: That they would gain wealth and comfort.
options.note: His prayer is for growing love and righteousness, not material ease.

options.text: That they would simply feel happy.
options.note: The aim is discerning love and fruit to God's glory, deeper than feeling.

options.text: That they would keep their love vague and untested.
options.note: He prays love would grow in knowledge to test and approve what is best.

quiz.q: Whom does Paul address as saints at Philippi?

options.text: All the believers there, together with the overseers and deacons.
options.note: Every believer in Christ is a saint, the whole church with its leaders.

options.text: Only a spiritual elite who had reached perfection.
options.note: Saint means set apart in Christ, not an elite class.

options.text: Only the overseers and deacons.
options.note: He greets all the saints, with the leaders alongside them.

options.text: People made holy by their own good works.
options.note: They are holy by being in Christ Jesus, not by achievement.

quiz.q: To what end does the fruit of righteousness finally point?

options.text: To the glory and praise of God.
options.note: Verse 11 makes God's glory the goal of the fruit that comes through Christ.

options.text: To the believer's reputation.
options.note: The fruit is real, but its aim is God's glory, not personal credit.

options.text: To earning a place in God's family.
options.note: The fruit comes through Jesus Christ; it flows from salvation rather than earning it.

options.text: To nothing in particular.
options.note: Paul names a clear goal, the glory and praise of God.

questions: Paul thanks God with joy every time he remembers this church. Who are the people whose partnership in the gospel you could thank God for today?
Verse 6 says God will complete the work He began in you. Where do you need to rest in His faithfulness rather than your own staying power?
Paul longs for the church with the affection of Christ. How might Christ's own heart reshape the way you feel toward fellow believers?
Paul prays for love that grows in knowledge and discernment. Where is your love in danger of being sincere but undiscerning?
The fruit of righteousness is for the glory and praise of God. How does that goal change why you pursue a holy life?

translationNotes.note: Servants renders douloi, bondservants or slaves; Paul and Timothy present themselves as wholly owned by Christ Jesus. The BSB sets the recipients on a new line; here it is joined with a space, with no change to wording.
Carry it on to completion translates epiteleo, to bring fully to an end; the subject throughout is God, so the guarantee of perseverance rests on Him.
Affection renders splanchna, literally the inward parts, an idiom for deep, visceral love; Paul feels it with the very affection of Christ Jesus.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Philippians 1:1-11 this session. Verse 1 joins the two BSB lines with a space; wording unchanged. No em or en dashes occur in this passage.
